scenarios.question: A team evaluates their FM using traditional ML metrics (F1 score, accuracy) on a classification task, but users still complain about response quality. What additional GenAI-specific metrics should they measure?
thinkPrompts: Why are traditional metrics insufficient for GenAI?
What new metrics matter (relevance, fluency, helpfulness)?
How do you create evaluation datasets?
scenarios.answer: Traditional metrics miss key GenAI quality dimensions. Add: 1) Relevance: does the response address the user's actual question? Measure via semantic similarity to ideal answers using LLM-as-judge scoring. 2) Factual accuracy: are claims correct? Compare against ground truth or source documents. 3) Helpfulness: does it solve the user's problem? Measure via user satisfaction ratings and task completion rates. 4) Fluency/coherence: is the response well-written and logically structured? 5) Completeness: does it cover all aspects of the question? 6) Honesty: does it acknowledge uncertainty when appropriate? Create an evaluation dataset of 200+ diverse queries with human-annotated quality scores across all dimensions. Run Bedrock Model Evaluations with both built-in and custom metrics. Track all dimensions over time in CloudWatch to catch dimension-specific regression.

scenarios.question: How do you create a high-quality evaluation dataset for a GenAI application when you do not have existing labeled data?
thinkPrompts: Where do evaluation queries come from?
How do you annotate ground truth?
How many examples do you need?
scenarios.answer: Evaluation dataset creation process: 1) Query collection: sample real user queries from production logs (or if pre-launch, conduct user research to identify likely queries). Include diverse categories: simple, complex, ambiguous, edge cases, adversarial. Target 200-500 queries. 2) Ground truth annotation: hire domain experts via SageMaker Ground Truth to write ideal reference answers for each query. Use multiple annotators (3+) and measure inter-annotator agreement. 3) Multi-dimensional scoring rubrics: define clear criteria for each quality dimension (1-5 scale for relevance, accuracy, helpfulness, fluency). Annotators score the FM outputs. 4) Diversity requirements: ensure representation across topics, complexity levels, user personas, and edge cases. 5) Maintenance: refresh 10-20% of the dataset quarterly to reflect evolving user needs. 6) Use this dataset for both human evaluation and to calibrate automated metrics (LLM-as-judge).

scenarios.question: A product manager asks you to set up Bedrock Model Evaluations for their customer support chatbot. They want to measure quality across multiple dimensions. Walk them through the setup.
thinkPrompts: How do you configure Bedrock Model Evaluations?
What built-in vs custom metrics are available?
How do you interpret the results?
scenarios.answer: Bedrock Model Evaluations setup: 1) Prepare evaluation dataset: create a JSONL file with 200+ representative customer queries and ideal reference answers. Include diverse scenarios (billing, technical support, product info, complaints). Upload to S3. 2) Configure evaluation job: select the model to evaluate, choose evaluation type (automatic with built-in metrics, or LLM-as-a-judge for custom criteria). 3) Built-in metrics: accuracy, robustness, and toxicity. Custom criteria via LLM-as-judge: define rubrics for helpfulness (1-5), completeness (1-5), tone appropriateness (1-5), and policy compliance (pass/fail). 4) Run evaluation and analyze results: review aggregate scores per metric, identify low-scoring query categories, and examine specific low-scoring examples to understand failure patterns. 5) Action plan: prioritize prompt improvements for the lowest-scoring categories. Re-run evaluation after changes to measure improvement. Schedule monthly evaluations for continuous quality tracking.

taskTitle: Implement evaluation systems for GenAI
description: Create systematic model evaluation systems to identify optimal configurations (for example, by using Amazon Bedrock Model Evaluations, A/B testing and canary testing of FMs, multi-model evaluation, cost-performance analysis to measure token efficiency, latency-to-quality ratios, and business outcomes).

scenarios.question: You need to compare Claude Sonnet, Claude Haiku, and Llama 3 for a summarization use case. How do you set up a systematic evaluation using Bedrock Model Evaluations?
thinkPrompts: How do Bedrock Model Evaluations work?
What metrics should you compare?
How do you ensure fair comparison?
scenarios.answer: Systematic model comparison: 1) Create a Bedrock Model Evaluation job: prepare a dataset of 200+ documents with human-written reference summaries in the required JSONL format. 2) Configure evaluation: select all three models, choose built-in metrics (ROUGE, BERTScore for summarization quality) plus custom metrics via LLM-as-judge (conciseness, key point coverage, factual accuracy). 3) Run the evaluation with identical prompts and parameters across all models. 4) Analyze results: compare quality scores, cost per summary (tokens * price), and latency. Calculate cost-per-quality-point for each model. 5) Present findings: Haiku may score 0.8 quality at $0.001/summary. Sonnet may score 0.92 at $0.01/summary. Llama may score 0.85 at $0.005/summary. 6) Recommendation depends on quality requirements and budget. Consider using Haiku for short documents and Sonnet for complex documents (tiered approach).

scenarios.question: You are rolling out a new version of Claude for your production application. How do you safely validate it using canary testing before full deployment?
thinkPrompts: What is canary testing for FM models?
What metrics trigger a rollback?
How do you route traffic progressively?
scenarios.answer: Canary testing for FM model updates: 1) Setup: deploy the new model version alongside the existing one. Use Lambda-based traffic routing. 2) Phase 1 (Canary): route 2% of traffic to the new model. Monitor for 24 hours. Compare quality metrics (from automated evaluation), latency, error rates, and user satisfaction against the baseline model. 3) Phase 2 (Linear rollout): if Phase 1 passes, increase to 10%, then 25%, then 50%, then 100%. Monitor for 4-6 hours at each stage. 4) Rollback triggers: automated rollback (via CodeDeploy or custom Lambda) if quality score drops >5%, error rate increases >1%, latency p95 increases >20%, or hallucination rate increases. 5) Quality gates: at each phase, run the full regression test suite against the new model. Block progression if any test fails. 6) Post-deployment: continue monitoring for 1 week. Keep the previous model configuration available for instant rollback.

taskTitle: Implement evaluation systems for GenAI
description: Develop user-centered evaluation mechanisms to continuously improve FM performance based on user experience (for example, by using feedback interfaces, rating systems for model outputs, annotation workflows to assess response quality).

scenarios.question: Your GenAI application has 10,000 daily users but only 50 provide feedback (0.5% rate). How do you increase the feedback collection rate and improve feedback quality?
thinkPrompts: How do you minimize friction in feedback interfaces?
What rating system captures useful data?
How do you close the feedback loop?
scenarios.answer: Increase feedback rate and quality: 1) Reduce friction: add inline thumbs up/down buttons directly in the response area (no page navigation). Enable one-click feedback without login. 2) Contextual prompting: after task completion, show a brief 'Was this helpful?' prompt. For negative feedback, show a dropdown of common issues (wrong answer, incomplete, too slow, off-topic) rather than free-text. 3) Implicit feedback signals: track copy/paste actions (positive signal), follow-up questions (negative signal), and session completion without feedback (neutral). 4) Incentives: show 'Help us improve' messaging and surface improvements made from past feedback. 5) Close the loop: when feedback leads to a fix, notify users ('Thanks to your feedback, we improved X'). 6) Target: 5-10% feedback rate. Store in DynamoDB linked to interaction ID. Analyze weekly to identify prompt improvements. Use high-quality feedback as ground truth for LLM-as-judge calibration.

scenarios.question: How do you set up an annotation workflow to create ground truth data from production FM interactions?
thinkPrompts: How does SageMaker Ground Truth work?
What annotation dimensions should you define?
How do you ensure annotation quality?
scenarios.answer: Annotation workflow setup: 1) Sampling: randomly select 200-500 FM interactions per week from production logs (stratified by query type, model used, and user satisfaction signal). 2) SageMaker Ground Truth: create a labeling job with a custom template. Define annotation dimensions: relevance (1-5), factual accuracy (correct/incorrect/partially correct), helpfulness (1-5), safety (safe/unsafe), and free-text correction field. 3) Annotator pool: use a mix of internal domain experts (for accuracy assessment) and crowd workers (for relevance and helpfulness). Require 3 annotators per item. 4) Quality control: include 10% golden items (pre-labeled) to measure annotator accuracy. Remove annotators below 80% agreement. Calculate inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha > 0.7). 5) Output: annotated dataset used for evaluation calibration, prompt improvement, and fine-tuning data. 6) Track annotation volume and quality metrics in a dashboard.

taskTitle: Implement evaluation systems for GenAI
description: Create systematic quality assurance processes to maintain consistent performance standards for FMs (for example, by using continuous evaluation workflows, regression testing for model outputs, automated quality gates for deployments).

scenarios.question: Your team deploys prompt changes every week but sometimes a change that looks good in testing degrades production quality. How do you implement automated regression testing and quality gates?
thinkPrompts: How do regression tests catch prompt quality issues?
What quality gates block bad deployments?
How does continuous evaluation work?
scenarios.answer: Automated regression testing and quality gates: 1) Regression test suite: maintain 500+ test cases covering all use cases, edge cases, and known past failures. Each test has input, expected output characteristics (not exact text), and quality dimensions to check. 2) CI/CD integration: add a CodeBuild stage that runs the full test suite against the new prompt version. Compare scores against the previous version's baseline. 3) Quality gates: block deployment if any metric drops more than 2% from baseline (relevance, accuracy, safety pass rate). Require zero failures on safety test cases. 4) Continuous evaluation: EventBridge triggers daily evaluation runs against production using the golden dataset subset. Track metrics in CloudWatch with anomaly detection. 5) Progressive rollout: even after passing quality gates, deploy to 5% canary first. Monitor for 4 hours before full rollout. 6) Rollback: if production quality metrics drop, automatically revert to previous prompt version via feature flag in AppConfig.

scenarios.question: How do you set up continuous evaluation workflows that run automatically and alert on quality degradation?
thinkPrompts: What triggers evaluations?
How do you compare against baselines?
What alerting thresholds are appropriate?
scenarios.answer: Continuous evaluation workflow: 1) Schedule: EventBridge rule triggers a Step Functions workflow daily at 6 AM. 2) Workflow steps: a) Select 100 golden dataset queries. b) Execute through production system (Lambda invokes the live API). c) Score responses using LLM-as-judge for relevance, accuracy, helpfulness. d) Compare scores against rolling 7-day baseline. e) Store results in DynamoDB and emit CloudWatch metrics. 3) Alerting: CloudWatch alarms for: average quality score drops >5% from baseline, any individual metric drops below absolute threshold, or safety test failures (zero tolerance). Alert via SNS to the team Slack channel. 4) Dashboard: trend chart of all quality dimensions, comparison table (today vs. 7-day avg vs. 30-day avg), and drill-down to specific failing queries. 5) Event-triggered evaluation: also run after any prompt change, model update, or knowledge base refresh.

taskTitle: Implement evaluation systems for GenAI
description: Develop comprehensive assessment systems to ensure thorough evaluation from multiple perspectives for FM outputs (for example, by using RAG evaluation, automated quality assessment with LLM-as-a-Judge techniques, human feedback collection interfaces).

scenarios.question: Your RAG application has good retrieval precision but users say answers are often wrong. How do you diagnose whether the issue is retrieval or generation using RAG-specific evaluation metrics?
thinkPrompts: What are the key RAG metrics (context relevance, faithfulness, answer relevance)?
How do you evaluate each pipeline component independently?
What is the difference between retrieval and generation failures?
scenarios.answer: RAG pipeline diagnosis using component-specific metrics: 1) Context Relevance: score retrieved documents for relevance to the query. If high (>0.8), retrieval is working. 2) Answer Faithfulness: score whether the FM response is grounded in the retrieved context. If low despite good context relevance, the FM is IGNORING or MISUSING the context (generation problem). This is your likely issue. 3) Answer Relevance: score whether the response addresses the actual question. 4) Diagnosis: high context relevance + low faithfulness = generation problem. Fix by: strengthening the system prompt ('Answer ONLY based on the provided context'), adding few-shot examples showing faithful responses, or using Bedrock Guardrails Contextual Grounding check. 5) Use LLM-as-a-judge to automate all three scores. Track each metric independently in CloudWatch. Build a RAG health dashboard showing the pipeline funnel.

scenarios.question: How does LLM-as-a-judge work in Amazon Bedrock, and how do you ensure the judge model is not biased?
thinkPrompts: How do you configure LLM-as-a-judge in Bedrock Evaluations?
What evaluation criteria and rubrics do you define?
How do you calibrate against human judgments?
scenarios.answer: LLM-as-a-judge in Bedrock: 1) Setup: in Bedrock Model Evaluations, select 'LLM-as-a-judge' evaluation type. Choose a powerful judge model (e.g., Claude Sonnet or Opus). Define evaluation criteria with detailed rubrics (e.g., 'Score 5: completely accurate and comprehensive. Score 1: factually incorrect or irrelevant'). 2) Evaluation: the judge model reads the query, the FM response, and optionally a reference answer, then scores based on your rubric. 3) Bias mitigation: a) Use multiple judge prompts with different phrasings and average scores. b) Periodically calibrate: have humans score the same 100 responses and compare with judge scores. Adjust rubrics until correlation >0.8. c) Use reference-based judging (provide ground truth) for factual tasks where possible. d) Consider using multiple different judge models and averaging. 4) Scale: LLM-as-judge can evaluate thousands of responses automatically, making it practical for continuous evaluation pipelines.

scenarios.question: Your team wants to combine automated LLM-as-a-judge evaluation with human evaluation for their GenAI application. How do you design a cost-effective evaluation strategy that uses both?
thinkPrompts: When is human evaluation necessary vs automated?
How do you calibrate LLM-as-judge against human scores?
What is the optimal split?
scenarios.answer: Combined evaluation strategy: 1) Automated evaluation (daily, all interactions): LLM-as-a-judge scores every production response on relevance, accuracy, and safety. RAG metrics (context relevance, faithfulness) calculated automatically. Results feed CloudWatch dashboards and alerts. Cost: ~$0.01 per evaluation. 2) Human evaluation (weekly, sampled): sample 100-200 interactions per week, stratified by automated score (over-sample low-scoring responses to focus expert time on problems). Human annotators score on nuanced dimensions (helpfulness, cultural sensitivity, business appropriateness) that automated metrics approximate poorly. 3) Calibration (monthly): compare human and LLM-as-judge scores on the same 100 responses. Calculate correlation. Adjust judge prompts and rubrics to improve alignment. Target >0.8 correlation. 4) Cost optimization: human evaluation only on the dimensions where automated evaluation is unreliable. Use automated evaluation to pre-filter, sending only uncertain cases to human reviewers. Expected cost: 90% cheaper than all-human evaluation with >95% coverage.

taskTitle: Implement evaluation systems for GenAI
description: Implement retrieval quality testing to evaluate and optimize information retrieval components for FM augmentation (for example, by using relevance scoring, context matching verification, retrieval latency measurements).

scenarios.question: Your RAG system achieves 0.9 precision@5 on your test set, but users complain about missing important documents in responses. What additional retrieval metrics should you track, and how do you improve?
thinkPrompts: What does high precision but user complaints suggest?
What about recall@k and NDCG?
How does context matching verification help?
scenarios.answer: High precision + user complaints suggests a recall problem -- the system returns relevant documents but misses important ones. Additional metrics: 1) Recall@k: what fraction of ALL relevant documents are in the top-k? If recall@5 is only 0.4, you are missing 60% of relevant documents. 2) NDCG: are the MOST relevant documents ranked highest? 3) MRR: how high is the first relevant result? 4) Context matching verification: for each query, verify the retrieved context actually contains the answer (not just topically related). 5) Improvement: increase k (retrieve more documents), implement hybrid search (keyword + vector) to catch documents missed by vector search alone, add query expansion to capture alternative phrasings, and use a reranker model to improve ordering. 6) Track all metrics in CloudWatch. Create a retrieval quality dashboard showing each metric's trend over time.

scenarios.question: How do you build an automated retrieval quality testing pipeline that runs on every knowledge base update?
thinkPrompts: What triggers the test?
What test dataset do you need?
How do you measure retrieval latency?
scenarios.answer: Automated retrieval testing pipeline: 1) Trigger: EventBridge detects Knowledge Base sync completion event. 2) Test dataset: maintain 100-200 queries with labeled relevant documents (document ID + relevance score). Update the test set when new document categories are added. 3) Pipeline (Step Functions): a) Execute each test query against the Knowledge Base retrieve API. b) Compare retrieved documents against labeled relevant documents. c) Calculate precision@k, recall@k, NDCG, and MRR. d) Measure retrieval latency percentiles. e) Compare all metrics against baseline from previous KB version. 4) Quality gate: if any metric drops >5% from baseline, block the KB update and alert the team. 5) Latency budget: retrieval must complete within 200ms at p95. Alert if exceeded. 6) Store results in S3, emit metrics to CloudWatch, and generate a comparison report sent via SNS.

taskTitle: Implement evaluation systems for GenAI
description: Develop agent performance frameworks to ensure that agents perform tasks correctly and efficiently (for example, by using task completion rate measurements, tool usage effectiveness evaluations, Amazon Bedrock Agent evaluations, reasoning quality assessment in multi-step workflows).

scenarios.question: Your Bedrock agent completes 85% of customer service tasks but you do not know why it fails on the other 15%. How do you evaluate and improve agent performance?
thinkPrompts: How do you define task completion?
What tool usage metrics reveal failure modes?
How does reasoning quality assessment help?
scenarios.answer: Agent performance evaluation: 1) Define task completion criteria per task type (e.g., 'order status' = correct order retrieved and status communicated; 'refund' = refund processed and confirmation sent). 2) Failure analysis: use Bedrock agent tracing to examine the 15% failures. Categorize failure modes: wrong tool selected (20% of failures), incorrect tool parameters (30%), tool error unhandled (15%), reasoning loop (10%), hallucinated information (25%). 3) Tool usage effectiveness: measure selection accuracy, parameter accuracy, and call efficiency per tool. 4) Reasoning quality: use LLM-as-judge on agent traces to score reasoning coherence, plan quality, and adaptation to unexpected results. 5) Improvement plan: fix tool descriptions to improve selection accuracy, add parameter validation in action groups, improve error recovery instructions in the agent prompt, add guardrails for hallucination-prone topics. 6) Track task completion rate per category in CloudWatch. Target: improve from 85% to 95% over 3 months.

scenarios.question: How do you evaluate whether a Bedrock agent uses its tools efficiently -- not making unnecessary calls or choosing the wrong tools?
thinkPrompts: What defines efficient tool usage?
How do you create a benchmark for tool selection?
What metrics matter?
scenarios.answer: Tool efficiency evaluation: 1) Create a labeled benchmark: 50+ tasks where domain experts define the optimal tool sequence (minimum necessary calls in correct order). 2) Metrics: Tool selection accuracy = (correct tool choices / total tool calls). Call efficiency = (optimal call count / actual call count). Parameter accuracy = (correct parameters / total parameters). Error recovery rate = (graceful recoveries / total tool errors). 3) Evaluation process: run benchmark tasks through the agent, compare actual tool usage against optimal sequences using agent traces. 4) Common inefficiencies: calling a search tool before checking the cache, using a powerful tool when a simpler one suffices, making redundant calls with slightly different parameters, not using tool results effectively (calling the tool but ignoring the output). 5) Improvement: refine tool descriptions to clarify when each tool should be used, add decision examples to the agent prompt, implement tool usage guardrails that limit redundant calls.

scenarios.question: How do you use Amazon Bedrock Agent evaluations to systematically test agent quality before deploying to production?
thinkPrompts: What does Bedrock Agent evaluation test?
How do you create agent test scenarios?
What metrics determine readiness for production?
scenarios.answer: Bedrock Agent evaluation for production readiness: 1) Test scenario creation: define 50+ tasks representing the full range of agent capabilities. Each task has: natural language instruction, expected tool usage sequence, expected outcome, and success criteria. Include edge cases (ambiguous requests, unavailable data, tool errors). 2) Run evaluation: execute all test scenarios through the agent. Capture traces for each execution. 3) Metrics: task completion rate (target >90%), tool selection accuracy (target >95%), average steps to completion, error recovery rate, and reasoning coherence score (LLM-as-judge on traces). 4) Quality gates: block production deployment if task completion rate <85%, if any safety-critical task fails, or if average steps exceed baseline by >30% (indicates inefficiency). 5) Regression testing: run the full suite after every agent prompt or tool description change. Compare against baseline. 6) Production monitoring: continue running a subset of test scenarios as synthetic canaries against the live agent.

taskTitle: Implement evaluation systems for GenAI
description: Create comprehensive reporting systems to communicate performance metrics and insights effectively to stakeholders for FM implementations (for example, by using visualization tools, automated reporting mechanisms, model comparison visualizations).

scenarios.question: Leadership asks for a monthly report on your GenAI application's performance, cost, and business impact. What should the report contain and how do you automate its generation?
thinkPrompts: What metrics matter for leadership vs engineering?
How do you automate report generation?
What visualizations support decision-making?
scenarios.answer: Automated monthly report: 1) Content sections: Executive Summary (key metrics, trends, recommendations), Quality (evaluation scores, hallucination rate, user satisfaction trend), Cost (total spend, cost per interaction, cost trend with projection), Usage (active users, queries per day, feature adoption), Business Impact (time saved, tickets deflected, estimated ROI). 2) Automation: Lambda triggered by EventBridge on the 1st of each month. Queries CloudWatch Metrics API for operational data, DynamoDB for user feedback, and S3/Athena for detailed analysis. Generates HTML report with charts using a Python charting library. Stores in S3 and sends via SES to stakeholders. 3) Visualizations: trend charts for quality and cost, model comparison radar chart (if evaluating new models), cost projection line chart, satisfaction score distribution. 4) Use QuickSight for interactive drill-down dashboards that complement the static monthly report.

scenarios.question: How do you build model comparison visualizations that help non-technical stakeholders make model selection decisions?
thinkPrompts: What visualization types support comparison?
How do you present cost vs quality tradeoffs?
What about side-by-side response examples?
scenarios.answer: Model comparison visualizations for stakeholders: 1) Radar chart: compare 3-5 models across dimensions (quality, speed, cost, safety, consistency) -- immediately shows each model's strengths and weaknesses. 2) Scatter plot: X-axis = cost per interaction, Y-axis = quality score. Each dot is a model. Clearly shows the cost-quality tradeoff frontier. 3) Side-by-side examples: select 5 representative queries and show each model's response alongside human ratings. This makes abstract quality scores tangible. 4) Trend chart: show each model's quality and cost over the past 3 months to demonstrate stability. 5) ROI table: for each model, project annual cost and business impact (based on quality metrics correlated with business outcomes). 6) Build in QuickSight with filters for stakeholders to explore (filter by use case, time period, query complexity). Include methodology notes and confidence intervals.

taskTitle: Implement evaluation systems for GenAI
description: Create deployment validation systems to maintain reliability during FM updates (for example, by using synthetic user workflows, AI-specific output validation for hallucination rates and semantic drift, automated quality checks to ensure response consistency).

scenarios.question: You are deploying a major model upgrade (Claude 3.5 to Claude 4) for your production GenAI application. How do you validate the deployment using synthetic user workflows and detect any regression?
thinkPrompts: How do synthetic workflows simulate real usage?
How do you detect semantic drift between model versions?
What quality checks run before and after?
scenarios.answer: Deployment validation for model upgrade: 1) Pre-deployment: run full regression test suite (500+ cases) against the new model in staging. Compare against baseline metrics. Block deployment if quality drops >2% on any dimension. 2) Synthetic user workflows: create 200+ synthetic interactions mimicking real user journeys (multi-turn conversations, RAG queries, agent tasks). Execute against the new deployment automatically. 3) Semantic drift detection: compare response embeddings between old and new model for the same 1000 queries. Calculate average cosine similarity. Alert if similarity < 0.85 (significant meaning shift). Also diff response length distributions, sentiment distributions, and topic coverage. 4) Progressive rollout: deploy to 2% canary, monitor for 6 hours. Check all quality metrics + synthetic workflow pass rate. If stable, progress to 10%, 25%, 50%, 100%. 5) Automated rollback: CodeDeploy monitors CloudWatch alarms. Auto-rollback if any quality metric breaches threshold. Keep previous model configuration for 30 days.

scenarios.question: How do you set up CloudWatch Synthetics canaries to continuously validate your GenAI API in production?
thinkPrompts: What are CloudWatch Synthetics canaries?
How do you write canaries for GenAI APIs?
What checks should the canary perform?
scenarios.answer: CloudWatch Synthetics for GenAI: 1) Create canary scripts (Node.js or Python) that simulate user interactions with your GenAI API at regular intervals (every 5 minutes). 2) Canary checks: a) Availability: API returns 200 OK within timeout. b) Latency: response time < SLA threshold. c) Quality: response contains expected structure (valid JSON, non-empty content). d) Safety: response does not contain known unsafe content markers. e) Functionality: submit a known test query and verify the response contains expected key phrases. 3) Configure CloudWatch alarms on canary success rate. Alert via SNS if success rate drops below 99%. 4) Multi-region canaries: deploy canaries from multiple regions to test global availability. 5) Integration: canary failures trigger PagerDuty incident. Results stored in S3 and visible in CloudWatch Synthetics dashboard. 6) Use canaries as the first detection layer -- they catch outages and quality issues within 5 minutes.

taskTitle: Troubleshoot GenAI applications
description: Resolve content handling issues to ensure that necessary information is processed completely in FM interactions (for example, by using context window overflow diagnostics, prompt design optimization, dynamic chunking strategies, truncation-related error analysis).

scenarios.question: Users report that your FM-based document analyst gives incomplete answers for long documents. Investigation shows the documents exceed the model's 200K token context window. How do you diagnose and fix this?
thinkPrompts: What happens when input exceeds context window?
How do dynamic chunking strategies help?
How do you prioritize content within token limits?
scenarios.answer: Context window overflow resolution: 1) Diagnosis: add token counting in the pre-processing Lambda (using tiktoken or model-specific tokenizer). Log token counts as CloudWatch metrics. Identify which component consumes the most tokens (system prompt, document content, conversation history). 2) For documents exceeding context: implement hierarchical chunking -- split the document by sections/headings, embed each chunk separately, and use RAG to retrieve only the most relevant chunks for each query. 3) Map-reduce pattern: for questions requiring the full document, chunk the document, run the FM on each chunk to extract relevant information, then synthesize the extractions in a final FM call. 4) Dynamic prompt construction: calculate available token budget (context window - system prompt - expected output), then fill with content prioritized by relevance. 5) Smart truncation: if truncation is needed, cut at paragraph boundaries, not mid-sentence. Log truncation events for monitoring.

scenarios.question: Your multi-turn chatbot starts giving poor responses after 10+ conversation turns. What is causing this and how do you fix it?
thinkPrompts: How does conversation history consume context window?
What are sliding window and summarization strategies?
How do you prioritize information?
scenarios.answer: Long conversation degradation is typically caused by conversation history consuming too much of the context window, pushing out system instructions and retrieved context. Fix with: 1) Token budget management: reserve fixed budgets for system prompt (500 tokens), retrieved context (2000 tokens), and user's latest message (500 tokens). Conversation history gets the remainder. 2) Sliding window: keep only the last N turns of full conversation history. 3) Summarization: after every 5 turns, use a fast model (Haiku) to summarize the conversation so far. Replace the full history with the summary. 4) Selective retrieval: store all turns in DynamoDB but only retrieve turns relevant to the current query (use embedding similarity to select relevant past turns). 5) Monitor: log the token allocation breakdown for each request as CloudWatch metrics. Set alarm when conversation history exceeds 60% of budget. Track response quality by turn number to detect degradation patterns.

scenarios.question: Your FM-powered document summarizer truncates important conclusions that appear at the end of long documents. The documents are within the context window limit but near it. How do you fix this?
thinkPrompts: Why does content near the end get lost?
What is the 'lost in the middle' problem?
How do dynamic chunking strategies help?
scenarios.answer: This is the 'lost in the middle' problem -- FMs pay more attention to the beginning and end of prompts but less to the middle. When documents are near the context limit, important content at the end competes with the generated output for token budget. Fixes: 1) max_tokens: ensure max_tokens is large enough for a complete summary. If the document uses 95% of the context window, only 5% remains for output. 2) Map-reduce summarization: chunk the document into 4-5 sections, summarize each section independently (each chunk is small enough for full attention), then synthesize a final summary from the section summaries. 3) Prompt structure: place the instruction 'Include key conclusions from the end of the document' AFTER the document content (leveraging recency bias). 4) Priority chunking: extract the introduction and conclusion sections separately and include them in a dedicated prompt section labeled 'Key Sections.' 5) Test: validate fix using documents with known conclusions and verify they appear in summaries.

taskTitle: Troubleshoot GenAI applications
description: Diagnose and resolve FM integration issues to identify and fix API integration problems specific to GenAI services (for example, by using error logging, request validation, response analysis).

scenarios.question: Your application is getting intermittent ThrottlingException errors from Bedrock during peak hours, causing user-facing failures. How do you diagnose the root cause and implement proper error handling?
thinkPrompts: What causes ThrottlingException in Bedrock?
How should you implement retry logic?
What monitoring catches throttling before users are affected?
scenarios.answer: Throttling diagnosis and resolution: 1) Root cause: you are exceeding the tokens-per-minute (TPM) or requests-per-minute (RPM) quota for the model. Check current quotas in Service Quotas console. 2) Diagnosis: query CloudWatch for Bedrock ThrottlingException metrics. Correlate with traffic patterns -- is it specific times, users, or features? Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to identify which requests are largest (most tokens). 3) Error handling: implement exponential backoff with jitter (start at 1s, max 30s, add random 0-1s). Use SQS as a buffer for non-real-time requests. 4) Prevention: request quota increase for production workloads. Implement client-side rate limiting (token bucket algorithm) at 80% of quota. Consider provisioned throughput for predictable capacity. 5) Monitoring: CloudWatch alarm on ThrottlingException count > 0. Dashboard showing TPM consumption vs. quota limit. Alert at 80% utilization before hitting the limit.

scenarios.question: Your Bedrock API integration returns empty responses intermittently but no error codes. How do you troubleshoot this?
thinkPrompts: What causes empty FM responses?
How does structured error logging help?
What response validation catches this?
scenarios.answer: Empty response troubleshooting: 1) Structured logging: log the complete request (prompt, model ID, parameters, token counts) and response (content, stop reason, token usage) for every invocation. 2) Analyze stop_reason in the response metadata: 'max_tokens' means the response was truncated (max_tokens set too low), 'end_turn' means normal completion, 'content_filtered' means guardrails blocked the response. 3) Common causes: max_tokens=1 or very low value (configuration error), guardrails blocking the response (check guardrail trace), prompt causing the model to produce empty output (test the prompt manually), or content filter set too aggressively. 4) Response validation: add post-processing Lambda that checks response is non-empty, meets minimum length, and contains expected structure. Return a graceful fallback message to the user when validation fails. 5) Monitoring: CloudWatch metric for empty_response_rate with alarm at >1%. X-Ray tracing to identify which request characteristics correlate with empty responses.

taskTitle: Troubleshoot GenAI applications
description: Troubleshoot prompt engineering problems to improve FM response quality and consistency beyond basic prompt adjustments (for example, by using prompt testing frameworks, version comparison, systematic refinement).

scenarios.question: Your FM produces well-formatted, fluent responses but they frequently do not answer the actual question. Users feel the model is 'confidently wrong.' How do you diagnose and fix this prompt engineering problem?
thinkPrompts: What causes the model to miss the question?
How do prompt testing frameworks help?
What systematic refinement process should you follow?
scenarios.answer: Systematic prompt troubleshooting: 1) Hypothesis: the system prompt instructions are too verbose and the model focuses on formatting over substance, OR the context window is overloaded and the actual question gets lost. 2) Test: create a test suite of 50 queries where the model misses the question. Run with different prompt variants: a) Shortened system prompt (remove formatting instructions). b) Question repeated at the end of the prompt (recency bias helps). c) Explicit instruction: 'First directly answer the question, then provide context.' 3) Measure: LLM-as-judge scores for 'answer relevance' (does it address the question?) across all variants. 4) Common fixes: restructure the prompt to put the question after the context (not before), use XML tags to separate context from question, add 'Answer the following question based on the provided context' as the final instruction. 5) Version comparison: track quality before and after the fix using the test suite. Deploy only if answer relevance improves without regression on other metrics.

scenarios.question: After updating a prompt template, 3 out of 20 test cases fail but the other 17 improved. How do you decide whether to deploy the change?
thinkPrompts: How does version comparison work?
What is acceptable regression?
How do you analyze the failures?
scenarios.answer: Systematic decision process: 1) Analyze the 3 failures: are they critical (safety issues, completely wrong answers) or marginal (slightly different format, minor quality decrease)? If any safety failures, do not deploy. 2) Categorize the improvement: did the 17 improvements address a known quality issue? How significant is the improvement (score increase)? 3) Quantify the tradeoff: calculate overall quality score change (weighted average across all 20 cases). If net positive and no safety regressions, the change is likely worthwhile. 4) Investigate the 3 failures: can you fix them with minor prompt adjustments without losing the 17 improvements? If yes, iterate. 5) If the tradeoff is acceptable, deploy with enhanced monitoring for the failure patterns. 6) Expand the test suite to 50+ cases covering the failure scenarios. Use this experience to improve the test suite for future changes. Document the decision rationale.

scenarios.question: Your prompt testing framework reveals that the FM handles English queries well but performs poorly on Spanish queries. The system is supposed to support both languages. How do you troubleshoot and fix this?
thinkPrompts: How do multilingual prompts affect FM performance?
What prompt adjustments help non-English queries?
How do you test across languages systematically?
scenarios.answer: Multilingual prompt troubleshooting: 1) Diagnosis: run the evaluation suite in both English and Spanish. Compare quality scores per dimension. Identify specific failure patterns: does the FM respond in the wrong language? Is factual accuracy lower? Are instructions being misinterpreted? 2) Common causes: system prompt is English-only (FM defaults to English processing patterns), RAG knowledge base has only English documents (no Spanish context to retrieve), or the embedding model handles Spanish poorly (retrieval failure). 3) Fixes: add explicit multilingual instructions to the system prompt ('Respond in the same language as the user query'). Add Spanish content to the knowledge base or implement cross-lingual retrieval. Test embedding model quality for Spanish (some models are multilingual, others are not). 4) If the FM itself handles Spanish poorly, consider a more multilingual model or a translation pattern (translate query to English, process, translate response back). 5) Expand the test suite with equal Spanish and English cases. Track quality metrics per language in CloudWatch.

taskTitle: Troubleshoot GenAI applications
description: Troubleshoot retrieval system issues to identify and resolve problems that affect information retrieval effectiveness for FM augmentation (for example, by using model response relevance analysis, embedding quality diagnostics, drift monitoring, vectorization issue resolution, chunking and preprocessing remediation, vector search performance optimization).

scenarios.question: Your RAG-based internal knowledge bot returns irrelevant documents for technical queries, even though the documents exist in the knowledge base. How do you troubleshoot the retrieval pipeline?
thinkPrompts: Is this a retrieval or generation problem?
How do embedding quality diagnostics help?
Could chunking be the issue?
scenarios.answer: RAG retrieval troubleshooting: 1) Isolate the problem: call the Knowledge Base retrieve API directly (without FM generation) with failing queries. If retrieved documents are irrelevant, it is a retrieval problem. 2) Embedding quality: check that the embedding model handles technical vocabulary. Test with a domain-specific query -- if the embedding does not capture technical meaning, consider fine-tuning the embedding model or using a domain-specific one. 3) Chunking analysis: examine the chunks for failing documents. If chunks split technical content across boundaries, key information may be fragmented. Increase chunk size or switch to semantic chunking that respects section boundaries. 4) Metadata filtering: if the knowledge base has mixed domains, add metadata filters to narrow search scope (e.g., department=engineering). 5) Hybrid search: enable keyword + vector hybrid search. Technical queries with specific terms benefit from keyword matching. 6) Query analysis: test if reformulating the query helps. If so, add query preprocessing (expansion, rewriting).

scenarios.question: Your vector store retrieval quality dropped after a knowledge base sync that added 50,000 new documents. Previous queries that worked now return irrelevant results. What happened?
thinkPrompts: What causes quality degradation after adding documents?
How does embedding drift affect results?
How do you diagnose vectorization issues?
scenarios.answer: Post-sync quality drop diagnosis: 1) Embedding model mismatch: verify the new documents were embedded with the SAME embedding model and version as existing documents. If a different model was used, embeddings are in different vector spaces -- this is the most common cause. Fix: re-embed all documents with a consistent model. 2) Chunk quality: check if the new documents have different formats (PDFs vs. HTML vs. Word) that the chunking strategy handles poorly. Inspect chunks for the new documents -- are they meaningful units? 3) Signal dilution: 50K new documents may have shifted the vector space. Previously unique technical terms now match broadly. Fix: add metadata filters to scope searches. 4) Index degradation: large batch ingestion without index optimization can degrade HNSW performance. Trigger an index rebuild/optimization. 5) Immediate mitigation: if critical, temporarily filter out new documents by ingestion date while investigating. Track retrieval metrics (precision@k, recall@k) before and after each KB sync as a standard practice.

scenarios.question: How do you monitor for embedding drift in production to catch retrieval quality issues before users report them?
thinkPrompts: What statistics detect drift?
How often should you check?
What automated actions should trigger on drift detection?
scenarios.answer: Embedding drift monitoring: 1) Baseline: when the vector store is first populated, compute statistics on embeddings: mean vector, variance per dimension, L2 norm distribution, and inter-document similarity distribution. Store as the baseline in S3. 2) Continuous monitoring: on each KB sync, compute the same statistics on new embeddings and compare against baseline. Lambda function calculates: drift score = statistical distance between new and baseline distributions. 3) Alert thresholds: if drift score exceeds 2 standard deviations, alert the team. If drift score exceeds 3 standard deviations, block the sync and investigate. 4) Retrieval quality canary: run 50 labeled test queries after every sync. Compare precision@k against baseline. Alert if it drops >5%. 5) Root cause toolkit: when drift is detected, check embedding model version, document format changes, and content domain shifts. 6) Prevention: version-tag every embedding with model ID. Reject ingestion if model ID does not match existing embeddings.

taskTitle: Troubleshoot GenAI applications
description: Troubleshoot prompt maintenance issues to continuously improve the performance of FM interactions (for example, by using template testing and CloudWatch Logs to diagnose prompt confusion, X-Ray to implement prompt observability pipelines, schema validation to detect format inconsistencies, systematic prompt refinement workflows).

scenarios.question: Your GenAI application's output format has gradually drifted -- JSON responses that were once well-structured now have missing fields and invalid formats. No prompt or model changes were made. How do you diagnose and fix this?
thinkPrompts: What causes format drift without prompt changes?
How does schema validation detect this?
How does X-Ray help trace the issue?
scenarios.answer: Format drift diagnosis: 1) Schema validation: add post-processing Lambda that validates every FM response against the expected JSON Schema. Log validation failures with the specific fields that failed. Track schema_compliance_rate in CloudWatch. 2) Root cause investigation via X-Ray: trace the prompt assembly pipeline to see exactly what the FM receives. Common causes of format drift without prompt changes: conversation context growing and pushing format instructions out of the context window, RAG context containing examples of the wrong format that the FM follows, or model provider updating the model version behind the API. 3) Diagnosis: use CloudWatch Logs Insights to correlate format failures with specific input characteristics (long conversations, specific retrieved documents). 4) Fix: pin format instructions at the END of the prompt (recency bias), add JSON Schema as an explicit constraint in the system prompt, implement constrained output generation if supported. 5) Prevention: schema validation as a mandatory post-processing step. Alert on compliance rate <99%.

scenarios.question: You need to implement a systematic prompt refinement workflow after discovering that a prompt template performs poorly on a set of edge cases. Walk through the process.
thinkPrompts: What does hypothesis-test-measure-iterate look like?
How do you fix edge cases without breaking normal cases?
What role does version comparison play?
scenarios.answer: Systematic prompt refinement: 1) Collect: gather 20+ examples of edge case failures with the current prompt (e.g., very short queries, queries with typos, ambiguous queries). 2) Categorize: group failures by type (format failure, wrong answer, empty response, hallucination). 3) Hypothesize: for each category, hypothesize why the prompt fails (e.g., 'ambiguous queries fail because the prompt does not instruct the model to ask for clarification'). 4) Test: modify the prompt to address one hypothesis at a time. Add instruction: 'If the query is ambiguous, ask for clarification before answering.' Run the modified prompt against: a) the edge case test set (should improve), b) the full regression test suite (should not regress). 5) Measure: compare quality scores for both sets. Use version comparison (S3 versioning) to track exactly what changed. 6) Iterate: if the fix helps edge cases but regresses normal cases, refine further. If tradeoff is acceptable (>80% edge case improvement, <2% normal case regression), deploy with monitoring. 7) Document: add the edge cases to the permanent test suite to prevent future regression.

scenarios.question: CloudWatch Logs show that your FM sometimes 'confuses' two different prompt templates, following instructions from template A when template B was invoked. How do you diagnose this?
thinkPrompts: How do prompt templates get confused?
How does X-Ray trace prompt assembly?
What about caching issues?
scenarios.answer: Prompt confusion diagnosis: 1) Enable X-Ray tracing on the prompt assembly pipeline. Trace each step: template resolution, variable substitution, context injection, and final prompt assembly. 2) Common causes: a) Caching bug: if prompts are cached by a partial key (e.g., session ID without template ID), one user's prompt could be served to another's request. Check ElastiCache/DynamoDB cache keys. b) Variable substitution error: template variables not properly isolated, causing template A's variables to leak into template B. c) Conversation history: if multi-turn, the history may contain responses from a different template interaction. 3) Fix: add template ID to every cache key, implement strict variable scoping in the template engine, and clear conversation history when switching templates. 4) Monitoring: add template_id as a CloudWatch metric dimension. Log the final assembled prompt (redacted) alongside the template_id. Alert when response characteristics do not match the expected template pattern.
